keyConcepts.label: The characteristic equation
keyConcepts.microLesson: A matrix $A$ usually rotates and stretches vectors. An **eigenvector** is a special direction that $A$ leaves pointing the same way, only scaled by a factor $\lambda$ (the **eigenvalue**): $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$. To find the $\lambda$ that allow this, you need $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ — the **characteristic equation**. For a $2\times 2$ matrix this expands to $\lambda^2 - (\text{trace})\lambda + \det = 0$.

keyConcepts.label: Finding the eigenvalues
keyConcepts.microLesson: The eigenvalues are exactly the roots of the characteristic polynomial. So once you have $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$, you just **solve that polynomial** — factor it or use the quadratic formula. For $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ the equation is $(2-\lambda)(3-\lambda)=0$, giving $\lambda = 2$ or $\lambda = 3$. A diagonal or triangular matrix wears its eigenvalues on its diagonal.

keyConcepts.label: Finding the eigenvectors
keyConcepts.microLesson: Each eigenvalue $\lambda$ has a direction attached. Plug it back in and solve $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ for $\mathbf{v}$. Because any scalar multiple of an eigenvector is also an eigenvector, the answer is a whole line of vectors — so we report the one with the **smallest positive integer components**. Always verify with $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$: the matrix really does just scale it.

keyConcepts.label: Reading eigenvalues in the real world
keyConcepts.microLesson: Eigen-structure is the hidden skeleton of a system. The **dominant eigenvector** (largest $\lambda$) is the long-run steady state of a Markov chain or the ranking in PageRank. The **sign of the eigenvalues** decides stability: all negative means a system settles, any positive means it blows up. In PCA the top eigenvector is the **principal direction** of the data; in mechanics eigenvalues are squared natural frequencies — the **vibration modes**.

lecture.body:
## The direction a matrix can't twist

Multiply a vector by a matrix and, in general, it gets **rotated and stretched** — it comes out pointing somewhere new. But almost every matrix has a few special directions it can only *stretch*, never turn. Those directions are **eigenvectors**, and the stretch factor for each is its **eigenvalue** $\lambda$. The defining relationship is beautifully simple:

$$A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}, \qquad \mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}.$$

Applying the whole matrix $A$ to $\mathbf{v}$ does nothing more than scaling by the single number $\lambda$. This is not a curiosity — it is *why* Google can rank the web, why an engineer knows whether a bridge will shake itself apart, and why a data scientist can compress a million columns down to two.

## Step 1: the characteristic equation

Rewrite $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$ as $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$. We want a **nonzero** $\mathbf{v}$, which can only happen if the matrix $A - \lambda I$ squashes some direction to zero — that is, if it is singular. A matrix is singular exactly when its determinant is zero, so:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0.$$

This is the **characteristic equation**. For a $2\times 2$ matrix it always expands to a tidy quadratic in $\lambda$:

$$\lambda^2 - (\operatorname{trace} A)\,\lambda + \det A = 0,$$

where the trace is the sum of the diagonal entries. Notice this is exactly the quadratic-equation machinery you already own.

## Step 2: solve for the eigenvalues

The eigenvalues are the **roots** of that polynomial. Take

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}.$$

The trace is $7$ and the determinant is $4\cdot 3 - 1\cdot 2 = 10$, so the characteristic equation is

$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (\lambda - 2)(\lambda - 5) = 0,$$

giving $\lambda = 2$ or $\lambda = 5$. (Shortcut: a diagonal or triangular matrix shows its eigenvalues right on the diagonal.)

## Step 3: find each eigenvector

For each eigenvalue, substitute it back and solve $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$. For $\lambda = 5$:

$$A - 5I = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad\text{so } -v_1 + v_2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{v} = \langle 1, 1 \rangle.$$

For $\lambda = 2$: $2v_1 + v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v} = \langle 1, -2 \rangle$. Any scalar multiple works, so we report the **smallest positive integer components**. Always check: $A\langle 1,1\rangle = \langle 5,5\rangle = 5\langle 1,1\rangle$. ✓

Seen in the plane, that check is the whole story: $A$ moves $\mathbf{v} = \langle 1, 1 \rangle$ along its own line — stretched by the factor 5, twisted not at all.

```plot
{"type":"cartesian","xRange":[-1,6],"yRange":[-1,6],"grid":true,"segments":[{"from":[-1,-1],"to":[6,6],"dashed":true,"color":"faint"},{"from":[0,0],"to":[5,5],"arrow":true,"label":"Av = <5,5>","color":"warn"},{"from":[0,0],"to":[1,1],"arrow":true,"label":"v = <1,1>","color":"accent"}],"caption":"For A = [[4,1],[2,3]], the eigenvector v = <1,1> is only stretched: Av = 5v stays on the dashed eigenline through the origin"}
```

## Why anyone cares

The payoff is that a complicated repeated process becomes trivial along its eigenvectors — applying $A$ a thousand times just multiplies by $\lambda^{1000}$. That single fact is the engine behind steady states of populations, the stability of feedback systems, the principal directions in PCA, and the natural vibration modes of any structure.

application.summary: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors expose what a repeated linear process does in the long run. The dominant eigenvector is the steady state of a Markov chain (and the heart of PageRank); the signs of the eigenvalues decide whether a system settles or blows up; the top eigenvector is the principal direction in PCA; and eigenvalues are squared natural frequencies in vibration analysis.
application.workedExample:
**Where does the population settle?**

A city splits into Downtown and Suburb. Each year 70% of downtowners stay and 30% leave for the suburb, while 40% of suburbanites move downtown and 60% stay. The transition matrix is

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}.$$

The long-run distribution is the eigenvector for the **dominant eigenvalue** $\lambda = 1$ (every column sums to 1, so $1$ is always an eigenvalue of a Markov matrix). Solving $(A - I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ gives $-0.3 v_1 + 0.4 v_2 = 0$, so $\mathbf{v} = \langle 4, 3 \rangle$. Normalizing, **4/7 of people end up downtown and 3/7 in the suburb**, no matter where they started. The other eigenvalue here is $0.3$; because it is less than 1, the transient differences shrink by 30% each year and wash out. Google's PageRank is exactly this idea on a graph of 100 billion web pages — the dominant eigenvector *is* the ranking.
domainNotes: **Data science:** PCA picks the eigenvector of the covariance matrix with the largest eigenvalue as the principal direction — the axis that captures the most variance.
**Engineering:** structures have natural vibration modes whose frequencies are square roots of eigenvalues; matching a driving force to one of them causes resonance.
**Economics/biology:** Markov and Leslie matrices have a dominant eigenvalue (growth rate) whose eigenvector is the stable long-run distribution.
**Control theory:** a linear system is stable only if every eigenvalue has a negative real part — a single positive eigenvalue means it diverges.

problems.prompt: Write the characteristic equation $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ for $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$.

steps.instruction: Using $\lambda^2 - (\text{trace})\lambda + \det = 0$, the trace is $2+3=5$ and the determinant is $6$. Write the characteristic equation.

problems.solutionNarrative: Trace $=5$, determinant $=6$, so the characteristic equation is $\lambda^2 - 5\lambda + 6 = 0$.

steps.instruction: Compute the trace (sum of diagonal entries).
Compute the determinant $ad - bc$.
Write the characteristic equation $\lambda^2 - (\text{trace})\lambda + \det = 0$.

problems.prompt: Form the characteristic equation by expanding $\det(A - \lambda I)$ directly for $A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -2 \\ 4 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$.

steps.instruction: Write the matrix $A - \lambda I$, then compute its determinant $(5-\lambda)(-1-\lambda) - (-2)(4)$. Multiply it out and set equal to zero as a polynomial in lambda.
Solve $\lambda^2 - 5\lambda + 6 = 0$ for the eigenvalues.
steps.hint: It factors as $(\lambda - 2)(\lambda - 3) = 0$; a diagonal matrix shows its eigenvalues on the diagonal.

steps.instruction: Write the characteristic equation (trace 7, determinant 10).
Solve for both eigenvalues.

problems.prompt: Find the eigenvalues of the $3\times 3$ upper-triangular matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & 7 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$.

steps.instruction: For a triangular matrix the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries. State the characteristic equation as a product set to zero.
steps.hint: $\det(A - \lambda I)$ of a triangular matrix is the product of $(\text{diagonal} - \lambda)$ terms.

steps.instruction: Give all three eigenvalues.

problems.solutionNarrative: A triangular matrix has its eigenvalues on the diagonal: $\lambda = 2, 3, 6$.

problems.prompt: For $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, find an eigenvector for $\lambda = 3$. Give the eigenvector with the smallest positive integer components.

steps.instruction: Solve $(A - 3I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ and give the eigenvector with smallest positive integer components as $\langle v_1, v_2 \rangle$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $(A-3I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ forces $v_1 = 0$, so the eigenvector is $\langle 0, 1 \rangle$. Check: $A\langle 0,1\rangle = \langle 0,3\rangle = 3\langle 0,1\rangle$.

problems.prompt: For $A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, find an eigenvector for $\lambda = 5$. Give the eigenvector with the smallest positive integer components.

steps.instruction: Form $A - 5I$ and write the equation its first row gives for $v_1, v_2$.
Give the eigenvector with smallest positive integer components as $\langle v_1, v_2 \rangle$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $A - 5I$ gives $-v_1 + v_2 = 0$, so $v_2 = v_1$ and the eigenvector is $\langle 1, 1 \rangle$. Check: $A\langle 1,1\rangle = \langle 5,5\rangle = 5\langle 1,1\rangle$.

problems.prompt: For $A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, find an eigenvector for the other eigenvalue $\lambda = 2$. Give the eigenvector with the smallest positive integer components.

steps.instruction: Form $A - 2I$ and write the equation its first row gives.
Choosing $v_1 = 1$ gives the smallest positive first component. Give the eigenvector $\langle v_1, v_2 \rangle$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $A - 2I$ gives $2v_1 + v_2 = 0$, so $v_2 = -2v_1$ and the eigenvector is $\langle 1, -2 \rangle$. Check: $A\langle 1,-2\rangle = \langle 2,-4\rangle = 2\langle 1,-2\rangle$.

problems.prompt: The symmetric matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ appears in many applications. Find its eigenvalues, then an eigenvector for the larger one.

steps.instruction: Write and solve the characteristic equation (trace 4, determinant 3) for both eigenvalues.
For $\lambda = 3$, form $A - 3I$ and give the eigenvector with smallest positive integer components as $\langle v_1, v_2 \rangle$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $\lambda = 1$ or $3$; for $\lambda = 3$, $-v_1 + v_2 = 0$ gives the eigenvector $\langle 1, 1 \rangle$.

problems.prompt: A linear system $\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}$ has eigenvalues $\lambda = -2$ and $\lambda = -5$. A system is stable (settles to rest) only if every eigenvalue is negative. Is this system stable?

steps.instruction: Both eigenvalues are negative. Is the system stable? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Stability requires all eigenvalues to be negative — here both are.

problems.solutionNarrative: Both eigenvalues $-2$ and $-5$ are negative, so every mode decays and the system is stable.

problems.prompt: A two-region migration model uses the Markov matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$. The long-run population distribution is the eigenvector for the dominant eigenvalue $\lambda = 1$. Find that steady-state direction with smallest positive integer components.

steps.instruction: Form $A - I$ and write the equation its first row gives ($-0.3 v_1 + 0.4 v_2 = 0$, i.e. $3v_1 = 4v_2$).
steps.hint: $A - I = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & -0.4 \end{bmatrix}$; multiply the top row by 10.

steps.instruction: Give the steady-state eigenvector with smallest positive integer components as $\langle v_1, v_2 \rangle$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $(A-I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ gives $3v_1 = 4v_2$, so the steady state is $\langle 4, 3 \rangle$: in the long run 4/7 of people live in region 1 and 3/7 in region 2.

problems.prompt: In PCA, a dataset has covariance matrix $C = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$. The principal direction is the eigenvector of the largest eigenvalue. Find the largest eigenvalue, then the principal direction.

steps.instruction: Write and solve the characteristic equation (trace 10, determinant 21) for both eigenvalues.
The largest eigenvalue is 7. Form $C - 7I$ and write the equation its first row gives.
Give the principal direction with smallest positive integer components as $\langle v_1, v_2 \rangle$.
steps.hint: $v_1 = v_2$, so $\langle 1, 1 \rangle$ — the data spreads most along the 45° line.

problems.solutionNarrative: Eigenvalues are 3 and 7; the largest, 7, has eigenvector $\langle 1, 1 \rangle$, so the principal direction is the 45° line where the data varies most.

problems.prompt: A control system has state matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda = 3$ and $\lambda = -1$. Stability requires every eigenvalue to be negative. How many of these eigenvalues are positive, and is the system stable?

steps.instruction: How many of the eigenvalues are positive? Type a number.
Because at least one eigenvalue is positive, is the system stable? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: A single positive eigenvalue makes the system diverge.

problems.solutionNarrative: One eigenvalue ($\lambda = 3$) is positive, so that mode grows without bound: the system is not stable.

problems.prompt: A vibrating system has stiffness/mass matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$, whose eigenvalues are the squared natural frequencies $\omega^2$. Find the eigenvalues (the values of $\omega^2$).

steps.instruction: This diagonal matrix shows its eigenvalues on the diagonal. Give both eigenvalues.
steps.hint: For a diagonal matrix the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries.

problems.solutionNarrative: A diagonal matrix has its eigenvalues on the diagonal, so $\omega^2 = 5$ and $\omega^2 = 8$ — the two natural modes of the system.

problems.prompt: For $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, form the characteristic equation and find the eigenvalues.

steps.instruction: Write the characteristic equation (trace 2, determinant $-3$).
Solve for both eigenvalues.

problems.prompt: For the diagonal matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, find an eigenvector for $\lambda = 6$. Give the eigenvector with the smallest positive integer components.

steps.instruction: Solve $(A - 6I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ and give the eigenvector as $\langle v_1, v_2 \rangle$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $(A-6I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ forces $v_2 = 0$, so the eigenvector is $\langle 1, 0 \rangle$. Check: $A\langle 1,0\rangle = \langle 6,0\rangle = 6\langle 1,0\rangle$.

problems.prompt: A predator-prey interaction year over year follows $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$. Find the characteristic equation, the eigenvalues, and an eigenvector for the dominant (largest) eigenvalue, which sets the long-run growth direction.

steps.instruction: Write the characteristic equation (this triangular matrix has trace 5, determinant 6).
steps.hint: $\lambda^2 - (\text{trace})\lambda + \det = 0$, or read the diagonal for a triangular matrix.

steps.instruction: Solve for both eigenvalues.
The dominant eigenvalue is 3. Form $A - 3I$ and give its eigenvector with smallest positive integer components as $\langle v_1, v_2 \rangle$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $\lambda^2 - 5\lambda + 6 = (\lambda-2)(\lambda-3) = 0$, so $\lambda = 2, 3$. For the dominant $\lambda = 3$, $A - 3I$ forces $v_2 = 0$, giving $\langle 1, 0 \rangle$ — the long-run population grows by a factor of 3 each year along that direction.
